prompt_text:
You are shown a SKELETON view of a python repository: class headers,
inheritance, method signatures, and attributes — but NO method bodies.

Use this signature-level view to detect structural violations of Open/Closed Principle (OCP):
- Fat interfaces / bloated classes (many unrelated method groups)
- Clients forced to implement methods they don't need (abstract methods / NotImplementedError / empty overrides)
- Inheritance hierarchies where subclasses alter the contract shape
- Broad APIs that ...
raw_response:
```json
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "file_path": "seaborn/categorical.py",
      "entity_name": "_CategoricalPlotter",
      "entity_type": "class",
      "line_start": 49,
      "line_end": 1360,
      "principle": "OCP",
      "severity": "high",
      "description": "Class contains numerous specific 'plot_X' methods for different categorical plot types.",
      "reasoning": "The _CategoricalPlotter class is responsible for orchestrating the drawing of many distinct categorical plot types (e.g., strips, swarms, boxes, violins, bars, points). Adding a new categorical plot type would require modifying this existing class by adding a new 'plot_X' method, thus violating the Open/Closed Principle. An OCP-compliant design would involve an abstract plotting interface and concrete strategy classes for each plot type, allowing new types to be added without modifying the core plotter."
    },
    {
      "file_path": "seaborn/distributions.py",
      "entity_name": "_DistributionPlotter",
      "entity_type": "class",
      "line_start": 99,
      "line_end": 1351,
      "principle": "OCP",
      "severity": "high",
      "description": "Class contains numerous specific 'plot_X' methods for different distribution plot types.",
      "reasoning": "Similar to _CategoricalPlotter, the _DistributionPlotter class is responsible for drawing various distribution plot types (e.g., univariate/bivariate histograms, densities, ECDFs, rugs). Adding a new distribution plot type would necessitate modifying this existing class by adding a new 'plot_X' method. This violates the Open/Closed Principle, as the class is not closed for modification when new behaviors are introduced. A more OCP-friendly approach would delegate specific plotting logic to polymorphic strategy objects."
    }
  ]
}
```

findings.description: Class contains numerous specific 'plot_X' methods for different categorical plot types.
findings.reasoning: The _CategoricalPlotter class is responsible for orchestrating the drawing of many distinct categorical plot types (e.g., strips, swarms, boxes, violins, bars, points). Adding a new categorical plot type would require modifying this existing class by adding a new 'plot_X' method, thus violating the Open/Closed Principle. An OCP-compliant design would involve an abstract plotting interface and concrete strategy classes for each plot type, allowing new types to be added without modifying the core plotter.

findings.description: Class contains numerous specific 'plot_X' methods for different distribution plot types.
findings.reasoning: Similar to _CategoricalPlotter, the _DistributionPlotter class is responsible for drawing various distribution plot types (e.g., univariate/bivariate histograms, densities, ECDFs, rugs). Adding a new distribution plot type would necessitate modifying this existing class by adding a new 'plot_X' method. This violates the Open/Closed Principle, as the class is not closed for modification when new behaviors are introduced. A more OCP-friendly approach would delegate specific plotting logic to polymorphic strategy objects.
